elements.sent-2411-001: We provide a simple framework for the study of parametric (multiplicative) noise, making use of scale parameters.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that for a large class of stochastic differential equations increasing the multiplicative noise intensity surprisingly causes the mass of the stationary probability distribution to become increasingly concentrated around the minima of the multiplicative noise term, whilst under quite general conditions exhibiting a kind of intermittent burst like jumps between these minima.
elements.sent-2411-003: If the multiplicative noise term has one zero this causes on-off intermittency.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our framework relies on first term expansions, which become more accurate for larger noise intensities.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work we show that the full width half maximum in addition to the maximum is appropriate for quantifying the stationary probability distribution (instead of the mean and variance, which are often undefined).
elements.sent-2411-006: We define a corresponding new kind of weak sense stationarity.
elements.sent-2411-007: We consider a double well potential as an example of application, demonstrating relevance to tipping points in noisy systems.
elements.sent-2411-008: Multiplicative noise (or parametric noise) is ubiquitous in real world systems and (in contrast to additive noise) is known to play an important role in state transitions \cite{horsthemke1984noise, feudel1997multistability, pisarchik2014control, feudel2008complex, kraut2002multistability, kraut1999preference, huerta2008experimental, de2007noise , including tipping points where hopping is induced from one attractor to another \cite{landa2000changes, forgoston2018primer, van1994noise, hodgkinson2021multiplicative .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Such events have relevance for example in synchronization \cite{kim1997noise, kim1997reentrant, lee1998phase, boccaletti2002synchronization , human balancing tasks \cite{cabrera2002off , financial crises \cite{krawiecki2002volatility, mandelbrot1997variation , environmental tipping points \cite{imkeller2001stochastic, RN4, majda1999models, majda2009normal, sardeshmukh2009reconciling, berner2017stochastic, sura2005multiplicative, buizza1999stochastic , neuroplasticity \cite{wang2022multiplicative, bauermann2019multiplicative , epidemics, and stochastic optimization \cite{pavlyukevich2007levy .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Another important noise induced phenomenon is that of on-off intermittency, where noise induces an aperiodic switching between static, so called laminar behavior and chaotic bursts \cite{platt1993off, heagy1994characterization, ding1997stability, elaskar2023review .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This is due to the noise causing the bifurcation parameter to fluctuate around the bifurcation point \cite{ashwin1994bubbling .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Such behavior is often observed as a continuous route from regular behavior to chaotic motion \cite{elaskar2023review, hramov2006ring, scheffer2009early .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Such intermittency has been observed for example in noisy laser systems, which may exhibit sporadic high intensity pulses\cite{young1988statistical, zhu1993steady, chowdhury2022extreme , Earth surface temperature at both weather and climate time scales \cite{schertzer1987physical, lovejoy2018spectra, ashwin2012tipping, sura2002noise and synchronization of coupled systems of interacting dynamical units, where the bursting induces a phase slip \cite{syncphillips23, gilpin2021desynchronization .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Theoretical work on the topic of noise induced transitions has generally taken two different approaches.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the first approach the effect of noise on a system is quantified according to the moments (mean, variance etc.) of the probability distribution \cite{schenzle1979multiplicative, aumaitre2007noise, bourret1973brownian, lindenberg1981brownian, lucke1985response .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This approach, while useful in a small noise or additive noise limit, is however not able to describe noise induced bifurcations, since unbiased noise does not affect the mean.
elements.sent-2411-017: The effect of noise on a system is instead to broaden and/ or to skew the distribution, generally leading to heavy tails and in some cases even leading to divergence of the moments.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the second class the sharp transition in the presence of noise is interpreted as the bifurcation of a maximum of the stationary probability density of a distribution \cite{horsthemke1984noise .}
elements.sent-2411-019: It remained unexplained, however, why only the maximum of the distribution should be observed in experiments \cite{graham1982stabilization .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this paper we explore a class of Langevin stochastic differential equations (SDE's), which we show under quite general conditions exhibit on-off intermittency.
elements.sent-2411-021: We build on the second approach suggesting the use of the maximum as an alternative to the mean and the Full Width Half Maximum (FWHM) as an alternative to the variance for systems with multiplicative noise.
elements.sent-2411-022: These two quantities collectively account for the body of the distribution excluding the heavy tail.
elements.sent-2411-023: We find counter intuitively that by skewing the tail of the distribution the noise induces a kind of stabilization of the minima of the noise term.
elements.sent-2411-024: It has been known for some time that unbiased parametric noise may stabilize an autonomous linear stochastic system of two or more dimensions in the sense of the moments, and that such noise is not sufficient to stabilize a one dimensional SDE \cite{arnold1983stabilization, arnold1979influence, bobrik1999stabilizing .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Here, however, we show that noise is sufficient to stabilize a one dimensional SDE in the sense of the maximum and the FWHM.
elements.sent-2411-026: The structure of this text is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-027: We first explore a general class of SDE's.
elements.sent-2411-028: We then focus on the noise stabilizing case where the trajectory turns out to exhibit on-off intermittency.
elements.sent-2411-029: We show how these systems can be analyzed in terms of scale parameters and how from this the FWHM can be derived.
elements.sent-2411-030: We then show why the mean is insufficient to arrive at conclusions about the systems with a finite number of trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-031: In the following section we explore the tails by considering first passage times (distribution of the bursts).
elements.sent-2411-032: We finish by generalizing the results and as an example of application exploring the effect of multiplicative noise in a double well potential.
elements.sent-2411-033: The general one dimensional Langevin SDE is \begin{equation}\dot{x} = f(x) + \sqrt{2D}g(x)\xi(t), \label{SDE}\end{equation} where $\xi(t)$ represents Gaussian white noise with mean $\langle \xi(t) \rangle = 0$ and correlation $\langle \xi(t)\xi(t') \rangle = \delta(t-t')$.
elements.sent-2411-034: We focus on the case where the drift $f(x)$ represents a non-confining potential, i.e. the trajectories drift toward infinity in the absence of noise.
elements.sent-2411-035: Due to the symmetry of the noise around zero and its lack of temporal correlations we notice that we may replace $g(x)$ with $|g(x)|$, and thus assume $g(x) \geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-036: For what follows we furthermore assume that $g(x)$ has only one zero.
elements.sent-2411-037: The probability distribution of Eq. (\ref{SDE ) evolves according to the Fokker Planck equation given by \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial t} p(x, t) = - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}[f(x)p(x, t)] + \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}[Dg(x)^2 p(x,t)].\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial t} p(x, t) = - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}[f(x)p(x, t)] + \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}[Dg(x)^2 p(x,t)].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: We first consider the case with natural boundaries.

u)D g(x_m)g'(x_m) = 0. \label{max}\end{equation} Here $
u = 1$ refers to the Stratonovich interpretation and $
u = 0$ to the Itô interpretation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: From this equation we see immediately that as $D$ increases the term $Dg(x)g'(x)$ becomes dominant, and thus that the $x_m$ moves closer to the zero of $g(x)$ as $D$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-041: This suggests that for large $D$ it may be sufficient to take the first term of a Taylor expansion around the solution of $g(x_0) = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-042: We expand $g(x)$ as a series and take the first non zero term of the expansion $g(x) \approx a_{m x^m$ (where $m$ is the order of the lowest non zero term of the series expansion).}
elements.sent-2411-043: The details of the higher order terms will often be unimportant, since we will be primarily interested in the case that the orbit spends most of the time in the vicinity of the minimum of $g(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Considering for the moment that $f(x)$ has a fixed point and $g(x)$ has a minimum at $x = 0$ (generalizations are discussed in the appendix) suggests studying the general class of first order Langevin SDE's \begin{equation}\dot{x} = ax^n + \sqrt{2D}x^{m}\xi(t) \label{sde_monomial}\end{equation} where the drift $a \in \mathbb{R _{>0}$ acts to create a non-confining potential.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We assume here natural boundary conditions (this choice will also prove to be unimportant in the vicinity of $x = 0$).
elements.sent-2411-046: It can be obtained easily from Eq. (\ref{max ) that the maximum of the stationary distribution changes with $D$ according to \begin{equation}x_m = \left(\frac{(2-\nu)m D}{a}\right)^\frac{1}{n-2m+1}. \label{max scale}\end{equation} We now obtain by straightforward calculation of the second derivative that the calculated extremum of Eq.~(\ref{sde_monomial ) is a maximum ($p''_{s (x_{max ) < 0$) in both Itô and Stratonovich interpretation if and only if the condition $n - 2m + 1 < 0$ holds.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: We note that these equations also holds exactly even if there is additional additive noise in Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ).}
elements.sent-2411-048: The stationary distribution of Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ) is given by \begin{equation}p_{s}(x;\beta) = Nx^{\nu-2m}\exp{\frac{a}{D}\frac{x^{n-2m+1}}{n-2m+1}}. \label{stat dist}\end{equation} This distribution is not always normalizable.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The normalization constant is calculated in Itô interpretation as \begin{equation}N^{-1} =  const. if $n - 2m < -1$ \infty if $n - 2m \geq -1$.  \label{self_sim_norm}\end{equation} This result also holds in Stratonovich interpretation provided $m > 1$ (See appendix for details).
elements.sent-2411-050: We now restrict ourselves to the study of Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ) in Itô interpretation $\nu = 0$, although we remark that qualitatively both interpretations lead to the same basic results.}
elements.sent-2411-051: We have so far seen that for $n - 2m + 1 < 0$ the stationary distribution has a single maximum and is normalizable.
elements.sent-2411-052: We now state furthermore in this regime that this distribution has a scale parameter $\beta$, i.e. \begin{equation}p_s(x;\beta) = \frac{p_s(x/\beta; 1)}{\beta}. \label{scaling dist 2}\end{equation} with scaling parameter given by \begin{equation}\beta = \left(D/a \right)^{\frac{1}{n-2m+1}}. \label{beta}\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \hspace*{-1cm}\includegraphics[width=9.3cm]{mon_tplot_prob_all_3.png}\caption{Time series and (unnormalized) stationary probability distribution for Eq.~(\ref{sde_monomial}) where $n = 0, a = 1$ and $m = 0.8, D = 0.4$ (a)-(b), $m = 0.5, D = 1$ (c)-(d), $m = 0.35, D = 5$ (e)-(f). For (d) and (f) $p_{s}(x)/N$ plotted (where $N$ is the normalization constant), since $p_{s}(x) = 0$. }\label{m03} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-053: The proof (outlined in detail in the appendix) follows easily from the stationary distribution.
elements.sent-2411-054: Using scale parameter properties we can obtain the effect of the parameter on the FWHM, the height and the cumulative probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-055: We start by noting that since the scale parameter serves only to change the scale of the distribution we must in general have a relationship of proportionality \begin{equation}x_{m} \sim \beta,\end{equation} which is indeed what we observe for both $D$ and $a$ comparing Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-056: We note that since $m$ for example is not a scale parameter we do not expect the same proportionality relationship to hold for varying $m$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Analogously the locations of the FWHM $x_{\pm H}$ compared to $x_{m}$ are clearly \begin{equation}p_s(x_{\pm H};\beta) \sim p_s(x_{\pm H}/\beta; 1) = \frac{1}{2}p_s(x_{m}/\beta; 1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: The cumulative distribution $F(x) = \int^x p(x')\,dx'$ is affected by the scale parameter according to \begin{equation}F(x;\beta) = F(x/\beta;1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: In the following we consider $n, m \geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The regime $n - 2m < -1$ ($\gamma < 0$) is shown in Fig. \ref{m03 (a)-(b).}
elements.sent-2411-062: This distribution does exist and is normalizable, however has diverging moments and a diverging tail (see appendix).
elements.sent-2411-063: This regime is characterized by a well defined finite maximum and FWHM, as well as on-off intermittency.
elements.sent-2411-064: In this regime increasing $D$ surprisingly causes the distribution to become more concentrated around the noise induced maximum both in the sense of the maximum $\sim D^{-|\gamma|}$ and the FWHM $\sim D^{-|\gamma|}$ of Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-065: It could be said that the noise has a more attracting effect.
elements.sent-2411-066: In fact (as shown in the appendix) as $D \rightarrow \infty$ the stationary distribution surprisingly converges to a delta function.
elements.sent-2411-067: This regime will be the focus of the remaining sections of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-068: We first briefly discuss the two other regimes.
elements.sent-2411-069: In the case of $n - 2m > -1$ (or $\gamma > 0$) it is clear from Eq.~(\ref{beta ) that increasing $D$ causes the stationary distribution to become more spread out over the non-confining potential in the sense of $x_m \sim D^{\gamma}$ and FWHM $\sim D^{\gamma}$ (see Fig. \ref{m03 (e)-(f)).}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In this case the extremum of the distribution, which can be obtained from Eq.~(\ref{max ) is a minimum.}
elements.sent-2411-071: As obtained in Eq.~(\ref{self_sim_norm ) the normalization constant becomes infinite and the particle consequently has probability zero of being in any particular location in the infinite time limit.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The boundary between the two discussed cases is at $n - 2m = -1$ ($\gamma = 0$).
elements.sent-2411-074: It is shown in the appendix that in Itô as well as in Stratonovich interpretation the noise induces a drift term $g(x)g'(x)$, which may counter act the drift term $f(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-075: The equality holds if and only if the deterministic drift term and the stochastic drift term have the same dependency on $x$, i.e. $f(x) \sim g(x)g'(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-076: This means that no maxima/minima can exist as can be seen from Eq.~(\ref{max ).}
elements.sent-2411-077: There is no maximum and no scale parameter.
elements.sent-2411-078: The distribution is in that sense 'scale' free.
elements.sent-2411-079: It has been shown by Kaulakys et al. \cite{kaulakys2006nonlinear, kaulakys2009modeling that such SDEs (with $n - 2m = -1$) correspond to $1/f$ noise.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This noise has the stationary distribution $p_{s (x) \sim 1/x^\lambda$ and spectral density $S(f) \sim 1/f^\alpha$ where $\alpha = (4m - 5 - a/D)/(2m - 2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Is the regime $n - 2m < -1$ of Eq.~(\ref{sde_monomial ) stationary? A (strict-sense) stationary process is one whose density function is invariant to time shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Typically this is taken to mean that the moments are all finite and are time independent.
elements.sent-2411-083: A wide-sense stationary process is one where first and second order properties are finite and independent of time.
elements.sent-2411-084: Generally this is understood to mean that the first two moments $\langle x(t) \rangle$ and $\langle x(t + \tau)x(t) \rangle$ are independent of time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-085: In the regime $n - 2m < -1$ of Eq.~(\ref{sde_monomial ) all of the moments $\langle x^l \rangle$ with $l = 1, 2, ..$ diverge to infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This is clear in Itô formalism since the evolution of the mean is the same a that of the deterministic system, which clearly diverges for $\dot{x = ax^n$ with $a > 0$ and $n \geq 0$ (and from this follows also the divergence of the higher moments).}
elements.sent-2411-087: Thus, in this sense (for $a > 0$) the distribution is not stationary, even in a wide sense.
elements.sent-2411-088: The distribution does however exist in this regime, as evidenced by Eq. (\ref{stat dist ).}
elements.sent-2411-089: For this reason we argue the moments are inappropriate to characterize the system of Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ).}
elements.sent-2411-090: If instead the first and second order properties are taken to be the maximum and FWHM then this distribution is in fact stationary in a wide sense.
elements.sent-2411-091: We will refer to this kind of wide-sense stationarity as M-wide sense stationary (meaning wide-sense stationary in terms of its maximum and FWHM).
elements.sent-2411-092: The distribution of Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ) when $n - 2m < -1$ is M-wide-sense stationary, but not wide-sense stationary in the sense of moments.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Let us now explore this type of stationarity in some more detail.

Red line shows theoretical mean value. (b): Divergence point of theoretical and measured mean (grey line in (a)).}\label{mean2} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-094: The trajectories of the SDE of Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ) when $n - 2m < -1$ exhibit bursts (see Fig. \ref{m03 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-095: If we observe a single trajectory, such as that of Fig. \ref{m03 (a) we see that regardless of initial conditions the particle will quickly return to the vicinity of the maximum.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Due to this, regardless of the initial condition, the correct maximum and FWHM can be established even for relatively short trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-097: This does not change over time.
elements.sent-2411-098: The mean on the other hand cannot in general be established for long trajectories (see Fig. \ref{mean2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-099: Due to the heavy tail of the probability distribution the larger bursts dominate the smaller bursts in size by orders of magnitude (Fig. \ref{mean2 (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-100: Since the probability of such a burst having happened increases over time, this means that for a finite ensemble of trajectories the probability of the bursting distorting the mean increases over time.
elements.sent-2411-101: This means that larger sample sizes are increasingly necessary to determine the mean accurately as time evolves.
elements.sent-2411-102: The fact that the trajectory keeps returning to the vicinity of the maximum is not in contradiction to the fact that the mean continually increases.
elements.sent-2411-103: This apparent contradiction can be explained by the fact that as time evolves, the probability of having had a large burst increases.
elements.sent-2411-104: To see why it is most appropriate to consider the distribution in terms of its maximum and FWHM instead of its moments, we run a large ensemble of trajectories (up to $N = 10^7$) of Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ) with initial conditions at $x_0 = 0$ (Fig. \ref{mean2 (b)).}}
elements.sent-2411-105: We see clearly that the average of a small ensemble may represent the theoretical mean faithfully only up to a certain time $t_{\text{div }$, after which the two values diverge.}
elements.sent-2411-106: This amount of time $t_{\text{div }$ can be clearly observed to increase logarithmically with system size $N$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: This means that it is unrealistic to describe any more than a fairly short trajectory $t_{\text{div \approx 10$ in terms of the mean using only standard computers.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: We now look at the statistical properties of the bursting for the on-off intermittency case $n-2m < -1$.
elements.sent-2411-109: The particle has a laminar length, defined as the amount of time (or length $l$) between two bursts.
elements.sent-2411-110: A burst is defined as quick event where the trajectory exceeds a certain threshold $x_c$.
elements.sent-2411-111: To calculate this distribution we begin with the time dependent Fokker-Planck equation of Eq. (\ref{sde_monomial ) is \begin{equation}\frac{\partial p(x, t)}{\partial t} = - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}[ax^n p(x, t)] + D\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}[x^{2m} p(x, t)]\end{equation} As the coordinate of the system stays for a long time in the region $x < x_c$, one can suppose that the probability density may find the form of a meta-stable distribution decaying for a long period of time.}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial p(x, t)}{\partial t} = - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}[ax^n p(x, t)] + D\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}[x^{2m} p(x, t)]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-112: The relaxation process of the probability density to this meta-stable state is supposed to be very fast in comparison with the time of the meta-stable distribution decay, therefore one can neglect the transient $0 \leq t \leq t_{tr}$ \cite{hramov2007length, hirsch1982theory, kye2000characteristic .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Under the assumptions above the probability density may be written as \begin{equation}p(x, t) = A(t)q(x)\end{equation} for small $x$.
elements.sent-2411-114: The decrease of $A(t)$ should be determined by the probability distribution taken in the boundary point $x_c$, i.e., $dA(t)/dt \sim -p(x_c, t)$.
elements.sent-2411-115: This assumption, which is also equivalent to neglecting the time correlation of the orbit, may be rewritten as \begin{equation}\frac{dA(t)}{dt} = -k A(t) \frac{1}{D x_{c}^{2m}}\exp{\frac{a}{D}\frac{x_{c}^{n-2m+1}}{n-2m+1}}\end{equation} where $k$ is a proportionality coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-116: In the absence of a further constraint it may be that both $k$ and $A(t)$ are functions of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-117: Evidently the decrease of $A(t)$ is described by the exponential law.. \begin{equation}A(t) = A(0)\exp{-k\eta t}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-119: For the purposes of further theoretical treatment we will explore the Mean First Passage Time (MFPT).
elements.sent-2411-120: The first passage time is defined as the time taken to travel from $x_0$ to the threshold $x_c$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Given that bursts quickly return back to the vicinity of $g(x) = 0$ we expect the distribution of laminar lengths to be equivalent to the distribution of first passage times.
elements.sent-2411-123: The mean of this distribution can be calculated using numerical methods (see appendix) considering reflecting boundaries at $x = 0$ as \cite{gardiner2009stochastic \begin{equation}T(x_0) = \frac{1}{D}\int_{x_0}^{x_c}\frac{dy}{\psi(y)}\int_{0}^{y}\frac{\psi(z)}{z^{2m}}\,dz \label{MFPT}\end{equation} where we have defined $\psi(x) \vcentcolon= \exp{\frac{a {D}\frac{x^{n-2m+1 {n-2m+1 }$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-153: In this section we apply the results of this paper to elucidate the role played by multiplicative noise in the tipping points in a double well potential.
elements.sent-2411-154: A classic model containing a double well potential (and multiplicative noise) is that of the (noisy) Duffing oscillator described by the equation \cite{aumaitre2007noise, bourret1973brownian, lindenberg1981brownian \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\gamma}\ddot{x} + \dot{x} = (a+y(t))x - bx^3,\end{equation} where $b$ is the (positive) friction coefficient and $d$ is the mean frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-155: This equation may for example be used to model the stability properties of the conductive state for Rayleigh Bénard convection, if we consider the Rayleigh number to be a noisy function of time \cite{ahlers1984externally, lucke1985response .}
elements.sent-2411-156: In the limits (i) $\gamma \gg \Delta \omega$ and (ii) $\Delta \omega \gg \gamma$ may both be represented by the same formal equation \begin{equation}\dot{x} = (a + \sqrt{2D} \xi(t))x - bx^3 \label{double well}\end{equation} if this equation is interpreted in the sense of Stratonovich in case (i) and in the sense of Itô in case (ii) \cite{graham1982stabilization .}
elements.sent-2411-157: This is the equation of a double well potential with multiplicative noise.
elements.sent-2411-158: In the absence of noise this double well potential has an unstable fixed point at $x = 0$ and two stable fixed points at $x = \pm \sqrt{a/b}$.
elements.sent-2411-159: The stationary probability distribution can be calculated as \begin{equation}p_{s}(x) = \frac{N}{2D}x^{\frac{a}{D}-2}\exp{-\frac{b}{2D}x^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-160: The FWHM is not simple to obtain from this equation, since by setting $p_{s (x_H) = p_{s (x_m)/2$ and calculating the maximum $x_m$ we obtain the $x_H$ at the point of the FWHM is a function of the non-analytic Lambert W function.}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Nevertheless, considering the ideas of this paper (comparing $(b/2D)x^2$ and $(x/\beta)^2$) we see immediately that we have a scale parameter $\beta \sim b^{-1/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-163: This agreement is illustrated in Fig. \ref{double_well_fwhm_unst .}
elements.sent-2411-164: We now explore the behavior of the system in the vicinity of these fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-165: In the vicinity of $x = 0$ we argue that noise may stabilize the system.
elements.sent-2411-166: To show this we first calculate the maximum of the stationary probability distribution using Eq. (\ref{max ) as $x = \pm \sqrt{(a-2D)/b}$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: As $D$ is made larger ($\rightarrow a/2$) the maximum moves closer toward $x = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-168: In this region we make a simplifying approximation of $-bx^3 + ax \approx ax^{1-\epsilon}$ where $\epsilon > 0$ is a small number and $\epsilon \rightarrow 0$ as $D \rightarrow a/2$.
elements.sent-2411-169: We can see immediately that $n = 1-\epsilon$ and $m = 1$ and thus $n - 2m < -1$.
elements.sent-2411-170: In other words the noise makes the fixed point at $x = 0$ M-stable.
elements.sent-2411-171: For $D > a/2$ the maximum of the stationary distribution is at $x = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-172: This again demonstrates the confining role of multiplicative noise, showing that white noise may stabilize the position of the potential well.
elements.sent-2411-173: If $D$ is small then we may linearize around $x = \sqrt{a/b}$ to obtain \begin{equation}\dot{x} \approx 2a\sqrt{a/b} - 2ax + \sqrt{2D}x\xi(t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-174: Using the substitution $y(x) = \alpha^{-1 x^\alpha$ where $\alpha = (D + 2a)/D$ we obtain \begin{equation}\dot{y} = 2a\sqrt{a/b}\left(\frac{D+2a}{D}y \right)^{\frac{2a}{D+2a}} + \sqrt{2D}\left(\frac{D+2a}{D}y\right)\xi(t).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-175: We see that $n = 0$ and $m = 1$ and thus $n - 2m = -2 < -1$.
elements.sent-2411-176: Thus these minima are also M-stable.
elements.sent-2411-177: We also see as noted previously that $b^{-1/2}$ is a scale parameter.
elements.sent-2411-178: While the fact that noise of a similar double well potential system causes the distribution to move toward zero at large intensities has been known from work from Graham et al \cite{graham1982stabilization that work focused on moments, such as $\langle x^2 \rangle$ related to the variance.}
elements.sent-2411-179: It was as such not able to account for the effect of the parameters on narrowing the peaks of the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-180: We have shown that under quite general conditions (when $n - 2m < -1$) increasing multiplicative noise intensity shifts the mass of the stationary distribution closer to the minimum of the absolute value of $g(x)$ and behaves as a scale parameter.
elements.sent-2411-181: We have shown that in this range there is exponentially distributed on-off intermittency, and that this goes hand in hand with the distribution having a scale parameter (a kind of self similarity).
elements.sent-2411-182: The fact that both of these phenomena are connected is well known in turbulence.
elements.sent-2411-183: The boundary case $n - 2m = -1$ is scale free power law noise (with a power law spectral density).
elements.sent-2411-184: When exploring the analytically most simple case of natural boundaries we have defined the concept of a distribution being $M$-wide sense stationary (having constant maximum and FWHM) and shown that the case of $n - 2m < -1$, even though it is not wide sense stationary in the traditional sense (having diverging moments).
elements.sent-2411-185: We have shown importantly that the FWHM generally varies with the scale parameters in this regime in the same way as the maximum.
elements.sent-2411-186: The maximum and FWHM act as analogues to the first and second moments and collectively describe the body of the distribution, excluding the heavy tail.
elements.sent-2411-187: The bursts have been considered using first passage time theory.
elements.sent-2411-188: Given essentially only the requirement of a significant multiplicative noise term we expect these results to be quite applicable to a range of different problems including extreme bursts, tipping points and synchronization.
elements.sent-2411-189: We have applied the ideas of this paper to a double well potential as a specific example.
elements.sent-2411-190: In particular these methods are important in the regime of large noise where standard small noise perturbation methods are insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-191: In this regime we have shown that it is sufficient to study the first term of a Taylor expansion of both the deterministic and stochastic terms of the Langevin equation.
elements.sent-2411-192: The authors have no conflict of interest to disclose.

elements.sent-2411-262: We assert that additional additive noise $D_2$ makes no difference to this behavior.
elements.sent-2411-263: To be more explicit the noise does broaden the distribution, making the FWHM wider.
elements.sent-2411-264: The FWHM is nevertheless made smaller again by increasing multiplicative noise according to $\sim D^{\frac{1 {n-2m+1 }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-265: The conditions required for the existence and uniqueness in a time interval $[t_0, T]$ are the Lipschitz condition and the growth condition.
elements.sent-2411-266: Almost every stochastic differential equation encountered in practice satisfies the Lipschitz condition since it is essentially a smoothness condition.
elements.sent-2411-267: The growth condition is that there exists a $K$ such that for all $t$ in the range $[t_0, T]$ \begin{equation}|f(x,t)|^2 + |g(x,t)|^2 \leq K^2 (1 + |x|^2)\end{equation} The growth condition in contrast to the Lipschitz condition is often violated.
elements.sent-2411-268: If these conditions hold then it is guaranteed that the solution will not become infinite in a finite time.
elements.sent-2411-269: For our case of $n = 0$ and $m = 1$ it is guaranteed that the solution will not explode.
elements.sent-2411-270: This is however not guaranteed if either $n > 1$ or $m > 1$.
elements.sent-2411-271: Simulations suggest that the trajectory does not explode over not too time frames for $n$ or $m$ slightly greater than $1$.
elements.sent-2411-272: Nevertheless it is for this reason that we generally restrict ourselves to the cases $n \leq 1$ and $m \leq 1$.

elements.sent-2411-274: Agreement seems to exist between conjecture and simulations even for smaller $D$, although simulations become increasingly more challenge in this domain, requiring a longer simulation time and a smaller time step.
elements.sent-2411-275: We also speculate that instead of higher order Gaussian moments such as the kurtosis, quantifying a heavy tailed distribution using the power law of the tail is more appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-276: The link between power laws and multiplicative noise has already been the work of recent literature \cite{levy1996power, sornette1997convergent, sornette1998multiplicative .}
